---
title: "From ISIS3 to Python: A Migration Feasibility Study"
subtitle: "Scope, Strategy, and Roadmap for Replacing USGS ISIS3 with a Python-Native Planetary Image Processing Stack"
author: "K.-Michael Aye"
date: 2026-04-19
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: sandstone
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: article
    papersize: a4
    geometry:
      - margin=2.5cm
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Motivation {#sec-motivation}

## The Rosetta 2 Deadline

USGS ISIS3 is a C++ framework that currently runs on Apple Silicon (M1–M4) only through
Rosetta 2 x86 emulation.
Apple is phasing out Rosetta 2 on the following timeline:

| Milestone | Date | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| macOS 26.4 | Feb 2026 | Rosetta deprecation warnings begin |
| macOS 27 | Fall 2026 | Last macOS with full Rosetta 2 support |
| macOS 28 | Fall 2027 | Rosetta 2 effectively removed |

After macOS 28, ISIS3 will not run on any Mac unless the USGS resolves its ARM-incompatible
dependencies.
This creates a hard deadline: by Fall 2027, macOS users need an alternative to ISIS3
for their planetary image processing workflows.

## Why Not Just Fix ISIS3 for ARM?

Native ARM compilation of ISIS3 is blocked by several dependencies that lack `osx-arm64`
conda packages (GitHub Issue
[#5188](https://github.com/DOI-USGS/ISIS3/issues/5188), closed as "inactive"):

- **Embree** — Intel's ray-tracing library, used for ray–body intersection with DTMs.
  Intel has not prioritized ARM support.
- **Bullet** — Physics engine used for collision detection.
  ARM builds are experimental.
- **Kakadu** — Proprietary JPEG 2000 library. No ARM builds from the vendor.
- **Version-pinned packages** — Qt (migrated from 5.15 to 6, which helps), Boost 1.72,
  and several USGS-maintained packages (cspice, SpiceQL).

An earlier issue requesting ARM support ([#4753](https://github.com/DOI-USGS/ISIS3/issues/4753),
Jan 2022) was also closed without resolution.

The Qt 5 → 6 migration is a positive step (Qt 6 has excellent ARM support), but Embree
and Kakadu remain fundamental blockers.
Removing these dependencies would require substantial refactoring of ISIS3's ray–body
intersection and JP2K support — work the USGS has not prioritized.

## The Case for Python

A Python-native stack avoids the ARM problem entirely: numpy, scipy, rasterio, pyproj,
and the CSM Python bindings (csmapi) all run natively on Apple Silicon today.
Beyond ARM compatibility, Python offers:

- **Lower barrier to entry** for planetary scientists who are increasingly Python-first
- **Smaller dependency footprint** — no Qt, no Embree, no Bullet
- **Composability** with the broader scientific Python ecosystem (xarray, dask, scikit-image)
- **Easier distribution** via pip/conda without building C++ toolchains


# ISIS3 Architecture Overview {#sec-architecture}

## Scale of the Codebase

ISIS3 contains approximately **367 CLI applications** organized as:

| Category | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Base (mission-independent) | ~199 | Core image processing, geometry, I/O, mosaicking |
| Control network tools | ~31 | Tie points, bundle adjustment, registration |
| Mission-specific | ~130 | Ingestion, calibration, instrument corrections |
| GUI applications | ~7 | qmos, qnet, qview, qtie, cneteditor, ipce |

## The IsisMain / Qt Pattern

Every ISIS3 application — even pure CLI tools — links against Qt because the parameter
system (`IsisAml`, ISIS Application Markup Language) uses Qt's XML parser internally.
The flow is:

1. The programmer writes an `IsisMain()` function (not `main()`)
2. The framework provides the real `main()`, initializing Qt and error handling
3. Parameters are defined in an XML file per app, parsed by `IsisAml`
4. `UserInterface` (inheriting `IsisAml`) handles both CLI and GUI modes

This means **every ISIS binary requires Qt**, even for trivial operations like
`stats input.cub`. This architectural choice compounds the ARM problem.

## Core C++ Libraries

### Camera Model Hierarchy

```
Spice
  └── Sensor
        └── Camera
              ├── FramingCamera       (e.g., MDIS-NAC, Galileo SSI)
              ├── LineScanCamera      (e.g., CTX, HiRISE, HRSC)
              ├── PushFrameCamera     (e.g., LROC WAC, Chandrayaan M3)
              ├── RadarCamera         (SAR instruments)
              ├── RollingShutterCamera
              └── CSMCamera           (CSM-compliant wrapper)
```

Mission-specific cameras (HiriseCamera, CtxCamera, etc.) inherit from the appropriate
intermediate class.
`CameraFactory` instantiates the correct subclass by reading plugin files.

### Other Key Libraries

- **Cube** — File I/O with PVL labels and binary blobs (Table, Polygon, History, Stretch)
- **Projection** hierarchy — `TProjection` (body-surface) and `RingPlaneProjection`
  with ~20 map projection implementations
- **ControlNet / ControlPoint / ControlMeasure** — Three-tier control network model,
  serialized as Protocol Buffers (version 5)
- **Spice** — NAIF kernel management, spacecraft state queries
- **Statistics / Histogram / MultivariateStatistics** — Statistical foundations

### Major C++ Dependencies

Beyond Qt, ISIS3 depends on: armadillo, bullet, cspice, eigen, embree, geos, GDAL,
GSL, HDF5, OpenCV, protobuf, nanoflann, PCL, qwt, SuiteSparse, SuperLU, Xerces-C,
usgscsm, ale, and SpiceQL.
This is a heavy dependency tree — which is precisely why ARM migration is so difficult.


# Complexity Tier Classification {#sec-tiers}

To assess migration feasibility, we classify all ~337 non-GUI ISIS3 applications into
four tiers based on the effort required for a Python port.

## Tier 1 — Trivial (~55 apps) {#sec-tier1}

Pure array operations achievable with numpy/scipy one-liners.
A Python port is essentially a numpy expression wrapped in a CLI.

**Arithmetic / pixel math:**
`algebra`, `ratio`, `fx`, `cubefunc`, `poly`, `specadd`

**Statistics:**
`stats`, `hist`, `percent`, `mvstats`, `cubeavg`

**Stretch / contrast:**
`stretch`, `gaussstretch`, `sigmastretch`, `histeq`, `histmatch`, `decorstretch`

**Geometry (array ops):**
`flip`, `mirror`, `rotate`, `crop`, `cropspecial`, `uncrop`, `enlarge`, `reduce`,
`translate`, `pad`, `center`

**Color space:**
`hsv2rgb`, `rgb2hsv`, `greyscale`

**Band management:**
`cubeit`, `explode`, `bandnorm`, `segment`

**Masking / trim:**
`mask`, `trim`, `bandtrim`, `circle`, `outline`, `specpix`

**Utility:**
`bit2bit`, `barscale`, `cathist`, `catlab`, `catoriglab`, `makecube`, `fakecube`

**Estimated effort:** 1–3 days each. The main work is handling ISIS cube I/O conventions
(special pixel values, PVL label propagation), not the algorithms themselves.

## Tier 2 — Moderate (~75 apps) {#sec-tier2}

Real algorithms, but existing Python libraries provide the core functionality.
The work is wiring up I/O, special-pixel handling, and ISIS conventions.

**Spatial filters** (scipy.ndimage / scikit-image):
`lowpass`, `highpass`, `gauss`, `median`, `sharpen`, `gradient`, `deriv`, `divfilter`,
`kuwahara`, `kernfilter`, `noisefilter`, `svfilter`, `mode`, `trimfilter`

**Spectral filters:**
`spechighpass`, `speclowpass`, `specdivfilter`

**Fourier domain** (numpy.fft):
`fft`, `ifft`, `butterworth`

**Map projection** (rasterio / pyproj — csm2map already covers cam2map):
`cam2map`, `map2map`, `map2cam`, `nocam2map`, `pixel2map`, `skymap`, `ringscam2map`,
`cam2cam`, `maptemplate`, `mappt`, `mapsize`, `mapgrid`, `maptrim`, `mosrange`, `maplab`

**Import / export** (GDAL / rasterio / pvl):
`isis2std`, `std2isis`, `isis2fits`, `fits2isis`, `isis2pds`, `pds2isis`, `isis2raw`,
`raw2isis`, `isis2ascii`, `ascii2isis`, `isis2gml`, `vicar2isis`, `isisexport`,
`isisimport`

**Mosaicking** (rasterio.merge + custom logic):
`automos`, `handmos`, `mapmos`, `noseam`, `blend`, `equalizer`, `tonematch`

**PCA / multivariate** (scikit-learn / numpy.linalg):
`pca`

**Stripe / noise removal:**
`dstripe`, `fillgap`, `lineeq`, `desmear`, `desmile`

**Label / metadata** (pvl):
`editlab`, `copylabel`, `cubeatt`, `getkey`, `trackextract`

**Registration** (scikit-image / OpenCV):
`coreg`, `findfeatures`

**Estimated effort:** 1–5 days each, depending on the amount of ISIS-convention handling.

## Tier 3 — Complex (~70 apps) {#sec-tier3}

Deep ISIS-specific algorithms with no drop-in Python replacement.
Porting requires understanding the algorithm, not just wrapping a library.

### SPICE / camera model initialization

`spiceinit`, `spicefit`, `csminit`, `caminfo`, `campt`, `camrange`, `camstats`,
`camtrim`, `camdev`

ALE partially replaces `spiceinit` for ISD generation, but the full `spiceinit`
includes kernel selection logic, label attachment, kernel database management, and
blob writing that ALE does not handle.

### Bundle adjustment

**`jigsaw`** — Full photogrammetric bundle adjustment.
Approximately 15,000 lines of C++ implementing sparse matrix solution (via SuiteSparse),
robust outlier rejection, parameter weighting, and error propagation.
No Python equivalent exists for planetary photogrammetry.
This is the single hardest app to port.

### Control network operations

`autoseed`, `cnetadd`, `cnetref`, `cnetcheck`, `cnetcombinept`, `cnetextract`,
`cnetmerge`, `cnetnewradii`, `cnetsplit`, `cnetstats`, `cnettable`, `cnetthinner`,
`cnetwinnow`, `pointreg`, `seedgrid`, `deltack`, `cnet2dem`

plio covers I/O (`from_isis()` / `to_isis()`), but none of the algorithmic operations.

### Photometric modeling

`photomet`, `photemplate`, `phocube`, `phoempglobal`, `phoemplocal`, `phohillier`,
`shade`, `shadow`, `shadowtau`

Implements Hapke, Minnaert, Lambert, Lunar-Lambert, and other photometric models.
No standard Python package provides these.

### Geometry (camera-dependent)

`noproj`, `warp`, `slither`, `appjit`, `findrx`, `remrx`, `framestitch`

### SPICE kernel writers

`ckwriter`, `spkwriter`

### Overlap / footprint operations

`findimageoverlaps`, `overlapstats`, `footprintinit`, `footprintmerge`, `getsn`

### Radiometric correction (instrument-specific)

Each instrument has a dedicated calibration app encoding years of detector-specific
knowledge: dark current models, flat fields, nonlinearity, temperature dependencies.

**Estimated effort:** weeks to months each.
Jigsaw alone would be a multi-person-year effort.

## Tier 4 — GUI Applications (~7 apps) {#sec-tier4}

Qt-based interactive tools requiring a fundamentally different approach for Python.

| ISIS App | Purpose | Python Status |
|---|---|---|
| **qmos** | Mosaic review | **Replaced** by isistools `MosaicReview` (Panel/Bokeh) |
| **qnet** | Control network editor | **Replaced** by isistools `TiepointReview` (Panel/Bokeh) |
| **qview** | General cube viewer | Not yet replaced |
| **qtie** | Tie-point editor | Not yet replaced |
| **cneteditor** | Tabular cnet editor | Not yet replaced |
| **ipce** | Integrated photogrammetric environment | Not yet replaced (USGS's own next-gen, still Qt/C++) |

The USGS built **IPCE** as the next-gen replacement for qmos/qnet/qtie, but it remains
Qt/C++ and therefore has the same ARM problem.
The isistools Panel/Bokeh/HoloViews approach is currently the only browser-based
alternative that runs natively on ARM.


# Existing Python Ecosystem Coverage {#sec-ecosystem}

## Well-Covered (Direct Replacement Exists)

| Python Package | ISIS Functionality Replaced | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **pvl** | PVL label parsing (catlab, getkey, editlab) | Excellent |
| **plio** | Control network I/O (cnetbin2pvl, cnetpvl2bin) | Good |
| **ale** | SPICE / ephemeris loading (partial spiceinit) | Good (~30 instruments) |
| **usgscsm + csmapi** | Camera models (campt, camrange, groundToImage) | Good |
| **rasterio / GDAL** | Cube I/O, format conversion, reprojection | Excellent |
| **pyproj** | Map projection definitions, coordinate transforms | Excellent |
| **scipy.ndimage** | All spatial filters | Excellent |
| **numpy** | All pixel math, statistics | Excellent |
| **numpy.fft** | Fourier domain operations | Excellent |
| **scikit-image** | Feature detection, registration | Good |
| **csm2map** (isistools) | cam2map | Production-quality |
| **isistools** | qmos, qnet | Production-quality |

## Partially Covered

| Area | What Python Has | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| spiceinit | ale generates ISDs | Kernel selection, label attachment, blob management |
| Control net algorithms | plio does I/O | autoseed, pointreg, cnetthinner — algorithmic operations |
| Photometric correction | Nothing standard | Hapke, Minnaert, Lunar-Lambert models |
| Mosaicking | rasterio.merge for simple cases | Tone matching (equalizer), seam removal (noseam) |

## Not Covered

| Area | ISIS Apps | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|
| Bundle adjustment | jigsaw | Multi-person-year — ~15K lines of sparse linear algebra |
| Radiometric calibration | All `*cal` apps (~50 total) | Instrument-specific; each encodes years of detector knowledge |
| Instrument ingestion | All `*2isis` apps (~40 total) | Each handles a specific raw PDS format |
| HiRISE pipeline | `hi*` apps (~25) | Deeply integrated chain of detector-specific corrections |

## Overall Coverage Estimate

| Tier | Python Coverage |
|---|---|
| Tier 1 (trivial) | ~95% — numpy/scipy can do all of these |
| Tier 2 (moderate) | ~70% — existing libraries cover the algorithms, ISIS conventions need wrapping |
| Tier 3 (complex) | ~15% — ale + usgscsm cover camera models; plio covers cnet I/O; algorithms remain |
| Tier 4 (GUI) | ~40% — isistools covers qmos/qnet; qview, ipce remain |
| **Overall** | **~35–40%** of base functionality has a viable Python path today |


# Strategic Assessment {#sec-strategy}

## The 80/20 Rule

About 20 apps account for ~80% of actual ISIS usage:
`spiceinit`, `cam2map`, `jigsaw`, `stats`, `crop`, `stretch`, `automos`, and the
`*cal` apps for active missions (CTX, HiRISE, LROC, CaSSIS).
csm2map already replaces the single most-used app.

## What Doesn't Need Replacing

ISIS3 will continue to run on Linux.
Tools that are:

- **used only in batch on HPC clusters** (jigsaw, autoseed on large networks), or
- **deeply entangled with ISIS internals** (spiceinit's full kernel management),

can remain ISIS-native, invoked via SSH, containers, or kalasiris subprocess calls.
The CSM ecosystem (ale + usgscsm) provides the interoperability bridge: a cube
processed through ISIS's `spiceinit` + `jigsaw` can be projected with csm2map on
a Mac.

## The Hybrid Approach

The most practical path is **not** a wholesale rewrite but an incremental migration
where Python tools replace ISIS apps one at a time, maintaining interoperability
through shared file formats (ISIS cubes, PDS products, CSM ISDs, GeoTIFFs).

```
                    ISIS (Linux / Rosetta)        Python (native ARM)
                    ─────────────────────        ────────────────────
Raw PDS EDR ──────► *2isis (ingestion)
                         │
                         ▼
                    spiceinit ──────────────► ale.loads() → ISD
                         │                        │
                         ▼                        ▼
                    *cal (calibration) ◄──── Python cal (future)
                         │
                         ▼
                    cam2map ◄───────────────► csm2map ✓
                         │                        │
                         ▼                        ▼
                    automos ◄───────────────► Python automos (future)
                         │                        │
                         ▼                        ▼
                    jigsaw (stays ISIS)       isistools review ✓
```

This approach:

- **Preserves investment** in existing ISIS workflows
- **Prioritizes the Mac-critical path** — projection, visualization, calibration
- **Defers the hardest targets** (jigsaw, spiceinit) indefinitely
- **Validates incrementally** — each new Python tool can be compared against its ISIS
  equivalent (as csm2map does with its `compare` command)

## Instrument Calibration: The Real Moat

The ~50 `*cal` apps each encode years of instrument-specific knowledge.
These cannot be auto-generated or mechanically translated — they require domain experts
for each instrument.
However, the algorithms are typically straightforward (dark subtraction, flat-field
division, I/F scaling) — the complexity is in the _calibration data and coefficients_,
not the code structure.


# Near-Term Roadmap: CTX End-to-End {#sec-roadmap}

The CTX (Context Camera) pipeline is one of the simplest in ISIS and a natural first
target for a complete Python replacement.

## The ISIS CTX Pipeline

```bash
mroctx2isis from=input.IMG to=raw.cub      # Ingest PDS EDR
spiceinit from=raw.cub                      # Attach SPICE kernels
ctxcal from=raw.cub to=cal.cub              # Radiometric calibration
ctxevenodd from=cal.cub to=clean.cub        # Even/odd column correction
cam2map from=clean.cub to=projected.cub     # Map projection
```

## Python Replacement Status

| Step | ISIS App | Python Status | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest | `mroctx2isis` | **Not started** — parse PDS3 label + raw binary | ~1 week |
| SPICE | `spiceinit` | **Covered** — `ale.loads()` generates ISD directly | Done (ale) |
| Calibrate | `ctxcal` | **Not started** — dark current, flat field, I/F | ~2 weeks |
| Even/odd fix | `ctxevenodd` | **Not started** — column-wise statistics | ~2 days |
| Project | `cam2map` | **Done** — csm2map | Done |

Total estimated effort for a full Python CTX pipeline: **~4 weeks**.

### ctxcal Algorithm Scope

The CTX calibration algorithm (in `isis/src/mro/apps/ctxcal/`) applies:

1. **Dark current subtraction** — uses overclock pixels from the cube's own buffer region
2. **Flat-field correction** — divides by a stored calibration flat image
3. **I/F conversion** — scales DN to radiance factor using solar distance at observation time

This is modest in algorithmic complexity.
The main porting work is locating and parsing the calibration data files (flats,
instrument constants) and handling ISIS special pixel propagation.

### mroctx2isis Algorithm Scope

CTX EDRs are PDS3 products with a straightforward format:

- PVL label (parsed with `pvl`)
- Raw binary image data (16-bit unsigned, line-prefixed with buffer pixels)
- The label contains all metadata needed for SPICE initialization

A Python ingestion function would: parse the PVL label, read the raw binary into a
numpy array, strip buffer/dark pixels, and either write an ISIS cube (via GDAL) or
keep the array in memory for the calibration step.

## Next Targets After CTX

| Instrument | Pipeline Complexity | Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cassini ISS** | Simple (ingest + cal) | ~3 weeks | Well-documented calibration |
| **Dawn Framing Camera** | Simple (ingest + cal) | ~2 weeks | Straightforward framing camera |
| **LROC NAC** | Moderate (cal + echo correction) | ~4 weeks | Linescan, similar to CTX |
| **HiRISE RED** | Complex (10 CCDs, ~25 pipeline steps) | ~3 months | Temperature-dependent dark models, channel normalization, jitter correction |

## Basic Mosaicking

A minimal `automos` replacement for map-projected cubes is straightforward:

1. Read GeoTIFFs with their affine transforms (rasterio)
2. Compute union bounding box
3. Composite with priority rules (input order or average)
4. Write output GeoTIFF with track band

`rasterio.merge` provides ~80% of this functionality.
The ISIS-specific additions (track bands, priority modes like "beneath"/"band"/"average",
null-pixel propagation) are moderate extensions.

Advanced features like tone matching (`equalizer`) and seam removal (`noseam`) can be
deferred to a later version.


# Long-Term Considerations {#sec-longterm}

## Jigsaw: The Strategic Bottleneck

Without a Python bundle adjuster for planetary photogrammetry, the control network
pipeline will always require ISIS (or ASP) for the adjustment step.
However, jigsaw operates on control networks and SPICE data — both of which are
accessible from Python (via plio and ale).
A Python jigsaw is conceivable but would be a multi-person-year effort requiring:

- Sparse matrix assembly and solution (scipy.sparse + CHOLMOD or similar)
- Robust outlier rejection
- Parameter weighting and error propagation
- Multi-observation geometric constraints

For the foreseeable future, jigsaw remains an ISIS/Linux tool.
The hybrid approach accommodates this: jigsaw runs on Linux, its output (adjusted
control networks and updated SPICE) is consumed by csm2map and isistools on Mac.

## The USGS Python Strategy

The USGS Astrogeology Science Center is itself moving toward a Python + CSM architecture.
[RFC 3](https://github.com/DOI-USGS/ISIS3/wiki/RFC-3-Planning-Document) (SPICE
Modularization) plans to move all SPICE kernel calls from ISIS C++ into ALE (Python),
estimating 500–1500 person-hours for the full migration.

The envisioned pipeline is:

```
PDS file → ALE → ISD → CSM → Ground Extents → GeoTIFF
```

This aligns exactly with the csm2map architecture.
The isistools project is positioned on the same trajectory as the USGS's own
long-term plans.

## GPU Acceleration

For compute-intensive operations (resampling in csm2map, large mosaics, bundle
adjustment), GPU acceleration via CuPy or custom CUDA/Metal kernels offers
significant speedup potential.
This is easier to achieve in Python (CuPy is a near-drop-in numpy replacement)
than in ISIS3's C++ codebase.

## Community and Sustainability

A Python-based stack lowers the contribution barrier significantly.
ISIS3 requires C++ expertise, Qt knowledge, and familiarity with a custom build
system.
Python tools can be contributed to by the broader planetary science community,
which is increasingly Python-literate.


# Summary {#sec-summary}

| Dimension | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Total ISIS3 apps** | ~367 (199 base + 31 control + 130 mission + 7 GUI) |
| **Trivially portable** | ~55 apps (numpy one-liners) |
| **Moderately portable** | ~75 apps (existing Python libraries) |
| **Hard to port** | ~70 apps (ISIS-specific algorithms) |
| **Already replaced** | cam2map (csm2map), qmos, qnet (isistools) |
| **Current Python coverage** | ~35–40% of base functionality |
| **Forcing deadline** | Fall 2027 (macOS 28 removes Rosetta 2) |
| **Recommended approach** | Hybrid — incremental replacement, ISIS stays for jigsaw/spiceinit |
| **Next milestone** | Full Python CTX pipeline (~4 weeks) |

The migration from ISIS3 to Python is not a single project but a multi-year ecosystem
shift — one that the USGS itself is pursuing.
The isistools project, with csm2map and the mosaic/tiepoint review tools, is already
contributing meaningfully to this transition.
The pragmatic path forward is incremental replacement of the most-used tools,
maintaining ISIS interoperability throughout, and prioritizing the workflows that
Mac users need most urgently.
